pages.raw_text:
The council. most of it, tried to
slip the proposed franchise of the
}. r. & N. across North Monroe
st. through last night. and would
nave succeeded were 1t not for
he hot attack made by Robert l..
Dalke. councilman from the Fourth
ward, who headed off the move
ment.


Captain John Gray submitted an
amended ordinance which differed
from the other in that it stipulated
that the o. R. & n. was to switch
cars only between 6 a m. and u,
and II to 6 p m. This practically
meant that switching might be
done at all hours of daylight, just
the time when Fourth warders
don't want it. No provision was
made for grade separation.


Councilman n. S. Pratt, following
Dalke. said that he would like 10
vote it down, but that Northslders
had not objected to the Great
Northern when it secured its fran
chise to cross several north side
street, including North Monroe
They offered not the least objec-
ton and the franchise was granted
with no strings on it, no grade sepa-
ration clause or provision as to just
when switching should be done.


Dalke became heated thereat and
said that Pratt was a party to an
infamous deal which allowed the
Great Northern to secure its fran
chise almost in a night and with
out the proper publicity.


Pratt retaliated by saying that
Dalke's declaration was false, and
explained that the franchise had
been advertised for 80 days.


Dalke insinuatad that Pratt was
a railroad worker. Pratt said that
was false.


The squabble ended by the mat
ter of grade separation being ar
gued pro and con to such an eX-
tent that it lost form and sense and
had to be referred to the corpora
ton counsel for an opinion as to
whether the city could. were
grade separation clause not in
cluded in the franchise, force it
upon the company Thefe was no
real need of referring the matter,
but it was a means of disposing.
temporarily, of the thing without
either councilman backing down.
